result.meeting_context: The Development Review Committee reviewed two projects: a proposed six‑story hotel with a two‑story office at 500 Ellis Street (East Whisman) and two new Public Storage buildings at 1040 Terra Bella Avenue. The session focused on site circulation, pedestrian engagement, architectural massing, materials, landscape canopy, and rooftop screening, with specific refinement recommendations and next‑step coordination with staff.

points.speaker: Tom Jess
points.text: The 500 Ellis proposal replaces two one‑story office buildings and surface parking with a six‑story hotel at Ellis/National and a two‑story office on Ellis. Centralized fully automated valet parking is enclosed, with limited surface ADA/oversize stalls, short‑ and long‑term bike parking, preserved perimeter trees, and a widened landscaped paseo exceeding precise plan expectations.
points.source: Tom Jess : The proposed site plan is for a six story hotel here on the corner... parking is central to the building... fully automated system... we are preserving trees around the perimeter... along here there's a landscaped paseo... we’ve really enhanced this and went above and beyond.

points.speaker: Tom Jess
points.text: Ground floors are highly transparent with a recessed outdoor patio along Ellis and an emphasized two‑story corner lobby. Hotel guest rooms wrap the central parking. The hotel’s first floor is tall (about 19.5 feet floor‑to‑floor), and the office building massing is simplified to assert a clear presence next to the taller hotel.
points.source: Tom Jess : large two-story lobby entrance here... tall glazing on the ground floor... we do have a a very tall ground floor... 19 point five floor to floor... office building... simplify the massing and make fewer, stronger statements.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: DRC requested rethinking the Ellis paseo entry to feel more open and welcoming, with clearer hierarchy and less stair dominance at the frontage. Members urged prioritizing a landing connected to the sidewalk and pushing ramps/stairs back to emphasize public access and place‑making over through‑circulation.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : I think this Paseo condition particularly at the access point at Ellis needs some, some rethinking... make it feel just a little bit more open and welcome... to encourage it as a a place not just to pass through.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: DRC raised concern that the secure bike room sits too far from Ellis, potentially encouraging wrong‑way riding in the drive aisle. The team should relocate or redesign bike access to ensure safe, direct, and convenient entry without forcing cyclists onto narrow sidewalks or into vehicle circulation.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : concerned about how far back from the Ellis St. frontage the secure bike room is... we don't want... bikes... coming the wrong way down that drive aisle... prioritize safe and convenient bike access.

points.speaker: Tom Jess
points.text: The architect agreed to soften the primary paseo entry by widening and shallowing stairs with landings and downplaying the secondary entry, clarifying public hierarchy. They acknowledged the feedback and will study placement to support a more welcoming, pedestrian‑oriented frontage.
points.source: Tom Jess : maybe widen this area and shallow the stairs up so that it's a couple stairs... this is clearly inviting and this side's a little bit downplayed so that there's a clear hierarchy.

points.speaker: Russell Sherman
points.text: DRC found the office building’s framed element reads as the most prominent feature, overshadowing the intended prominent lobby entry at the paseo. The committee requested adjustments so the entry clearly reads as the focal point, aligning elevation treatment with the stated massing diagram.
points.source: Russell Sherman : the framed element is the prominent piece... if it is the prominent corner that somehow they are handled in a way that makes them prominent.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: The office ground plane along Ellis should create stronger pedestrian interest and relate to the northeast corner strategy. Despite large windows, the base reads plain; the team should use materials/landscape to enrich the base and reconnect it with the massing diagram intent.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : the building base felt a little bit too plain... ask... what's the sort of material and or landscape design that's going to create that sort of pedestrian interest... overall... look at how you're creating material interest and contrast.

points.speaker: Tom Jess
points.text: The architect agreed to simplify and unify the office elevations so the promenade entry language carries across the paseo frontage, using material changes rather than added height to draw focus, and acknowledged the current entry treatment skews toward a suburban retail vocabulary.
points.source: Tom Jess : we can do something with materiality here... simplifying this... draws your eye to this entry... maybe this just feels a little suburban... like maybe the entrance to a Pottery Barn.

topics.topic: Hotel massing and key corner emphasis

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: DRC concluded the hotel’s street wall is over‑segmented into three vertical elements, diluting the key corner tower. They requested simplifying the elevation to read as a single plane that leads the eye to the corner, aligning with the massing diagram while retaining required plane breaks subtly.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : what we're actually reading is... a series of masses that... detracts attention from the key corner... study ways to... simplify the design... let the key corner stand out.

points.speaker: Russell Sherman
points.text: Recommendations included limiting vertical window grouping to the tower, regrouping windows elsewhere to avoid emphasizing vertical breaks, potentially increasing glazing on the recessed upper two stories to reduce visual weight, and bringing the awning down to visually connect the tower and lobby volume.
points.source: Russell Sherman : leave [vertical] to the tower... grouping of the windows... more glazing on that upper portion separates it... if you brought the awning down... it would make the tower feel more vertical.

points.speaker: Tom Jess
points.text: The architect agreed to consolidate the street wall plane, simplify colors, adjust window groupings to reserve strong verticality for the corner tower, and explore additional detailing at the recessed patio and café entrance to add interest without fragmenting the overall massing.
points.source: Tom Jess : simplifying the massing... leave [vertical windows] to the tower... simplify the colors... we have the recessed patio... café entrance... add some interest... simplifying the massing... like the diagram does draw your eye more towards here.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: The team intends a true commissioned mural on the blank face screening the automated parking, not decorative CMU. DRC encouraged creative window detailing to add depth and reduce a generic “hotel look,” and asked the landscape team to help highlight the key corner ground plane.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : is the intent... to work with an artist... Tom: we would like to work with an artist... encouraged... creativity in the window design... bring in your landscape team... to highlight... the main corner.

points.speaker: Tom Jess
points.text: A rooftop deck over the parking is not feasible due to structural limits (parking roof is non‑load bearing), biofiltration conflicts, and code separation between the steel hotel structure and the wood‑frame building. The roof will accommodate solar and equipment within constraints.
points.source: Tom Jess : structural engineer... it's a parking structure with a non load bearing roof... looked at... bio filtration... there is a firewall separation... this is an all steel... this is a wood frame... we'll be able to put some solar on it.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: Staff encouraged iterative, schematic‑level exchanges with the project planner to refine massing and frontage revisions before entitlement hearings, avoiding large rework later. Presenting updated elevations and landscape changes prior to hearings will help align expectations and address Council’s landscape priorities.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : we encourage you to work with your your project planner... iteration with staff is is really beneficial... pick up some of these... ahead of the public hearings... trees and landscape has been a high priority topic.

points.speaker: Tom Jess
points.text: The architect agreed to coordinate closely with staff on schematic refinements and praised staff support to date, specifically recognizing planner Ellen’s contributions to the project’s progress.
points.source: Tom Jess : Absolutely. Will do... it's been... great... working with Ellen. She's a superstar and we really appreciate all the help.

topics.topic: Terra Bella project overview and metrics

points.speaker: Bryan Miranda
points.text: Public Storage is partnering with Alta Housing by donating roughly half an acre and Terra Bella frontage, reentitling the balance for two storage buildings (phase 1: six stories ~84 feet; phase 2: four stories ~63 feet), totaling just over 400,000 square feet at an FAR just under 2.5.
points.source: Bryan Miranda : we give 1/2 an acre of our property... Heights... 84 feet for the six Storey and 63 feet for the four Storey... Total building area just over 400,000 square feet... the FAR is just under 2 1/2.

points.speaker: Bryan Miranda
points.text: Previous Planning Commission and City Council study sessions showed unanimous support for the architectural direction. The applicant aims for Planning Commission and City Council hearings early next year, subject to market conditions affecting Phase 2 timing.
points.source: Bryan Miranda : we had unanimous support for the architectural design... sail off to our Planning Commission hearing and City Council hearing early next year... subject to market conditions whether or not the four story will be built.

points.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
points.text: Since the last DRC, the team simplified the split‑face CMU base, made south elevations more symmetrical by aligning inverted L elements with massing steps, enlarged and mirrored the mural zone on the west frontage, and aligned ground‑floor storefronts with upper vertical rhythms on Building 2.
points.source: Jonathan Thomas : simplified that on both buildings... made them mirrors... made that mural larger... shifted [storefront] to be more in line and balance.

points.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
points.text: Rooftop mechanical is grouped into setback zones and screened twice: by tall parapets and 6‑foot corrugated metal screens coordinated with façade materials. Units are assumed under five feet; screen heights are conservative to ensure ground‑level invisibility under varied viewpoints.
points.source: Jonathan Thomas : grouping MEP equipment... set back... showing 6 foot screens... none of the units... over 5 feet... the corrugated panels... are actually going to be similar in nature to the facade materials.

topics.topic: Terra Bella landscape and parking changes

points.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
points.text: To increase canopy and improve screening, Building 1 removed four parking stalls, pulled back the trash enclosure, added trees including species suited to bioretention areas, and introduced smaller‑rooted trees along the north property line within the easement. Similar tree additions and gate setback were implemented at Building 2.
points.source: Jonathan Thomas : reduced 4 stalls on building 1... pulled this trash enclosure back... added more trees... specified a species... added trees along that N property line... Building 2... removed all of the parking stalls along the furthest eastern side... pulled the gate back.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: DRC recommended adding intermittent larger canopy trees along the highway sound wall near Building 1 to complement taller trees nearer Linda Vista, and exploring urban tree‑grate plantings along the east sidewalk where a nine‑foot path exists, while maintaining utility clearances (e.g., transformer access).
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : find opportunities to... intersperse a tree that's a bit taller... closest to Linda Vista... another opportunity... a tree great style tree planting... make sure... confident that that tree can be planted there [by transformer].

topics.topic: Terra Bella frontage, gate, and utilities

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: For Building 2, the DRC asked that the security gate be set behind the building face and better relate to the wrapped storefront corner so the architecture, not the fence, dominates the street view. The team will study further inset while coordinating frontage operations.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : ensure that... the security gate and fencing isn't forward of the building face... correspond to the the building architecture... study whether it's it's feasible to inset it further.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: Given uncertain Phase 2 timing, staff recommended either installing Phase 2 street‑facing tree plantings during Phase 1 near the temporary office, or at least interim planting along San Rafael, recognizing grading and future sidewalk constraints may require later relocation.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : we would like to to see the phase two... frontage planting incorporated into the the phase one... barring that... work with staff on on interim planting along that frontage... grading... sidewalk zone... may need to be removed or relocated.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: Staff emphasized early coordination on above‑ground utilities and water lines at building permit, to preserve planting areas and provide effective landscape screening for transformers and related equipment along public frontages, given typical clearance constraints.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : work closely with planning staff on the utility layout and make adjustments as needed to you know maximize the planting area and screening of those above ground equipment locations along frontages.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: The committee found the purple accent reads too pastel next to orange in renderings and asked the team to evaluate a darker, more saturated purple or a neutral dark gray alternative during on‑site mock‑up review, deferring the final choice to field confirmation.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : the purple maybe is a little bit too, too light... recommend... evaluate the the purple through a mock up... with an alternative, you know neutral, neutral, grey accent as an alternative.

points.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
points.text: On Building 2’s south elevation, DRC requested widening the central plaster panel by pushing the inverted L elements farther apart, and refining the double‑reveal corner proportion to match other locations. The team agreed these adjustments would improve rhythm and balance.
points.source: Jonathan Thomas : we would take that opportunity... reduce this and do something more along the lines of what happens like here... Rebecca Shapiro : push those LS a little further apart... get a nicer rhythm... double reveal panel... proportion there as well.

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: Spandrel glass should be applied to the back face within the window system and be as recessed as possible to maintain depth. Staff will verify this at permit to avoid later substitutions that push spandrel forward in the assembly.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : make sure that the the opaque spandrel application occurs on the... back face of the window systems... recessed as much as possible in the in the system.

topics.topic: Terra Bella solar and screening compliance

points.speaker: Rebecca Shapiro
points.text: New buildings must fully screen rooftop equipment; staff requested confirmation that solar panel racking will not exceed the lowest parapet height and that equipment sits well inboard. Conditions will reflect these requirements during final design and permit review.
points.source: Rebecca Shapiro : solar... structure... looked like it was taller than the the the parapets... I will ask... to make sure that that's sort of reflected in our design conditions... encourage... inset as much as possible.

points.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
points.text: The team confirmed parapets are tall enough to screen low‑profile solar arrays and that 6‑foot architectural screens and setbacks will conceal grouped HVAC units, with conservative heights ensuring invisibility from ground viewpoints.
points.source: Jonathan Thomas : those will definitely be lower than any roof screening... we have pretty tall parapets... showing 6 foot screens... set back... should... be covered over the solar panel system.

decisions.text: Rethink and redesign the Ellis paseo entry to be more open and welcoming, clarifying hierarchy and softening stairs with landings, coordinating with landscape before the next staff review.
decisions.source: Tom Jess : maybe widen this area and shallow the stairs... so that this is clearly inviting... Rebecca Shapiro : this Paseo condition... needs some rethinking.

decisions.text: Reevaluate secure bike room location and access to prioritize safe, direct cyclist entry from Ellis and avoid wrong‑way riding in the driveway.
decisions.source: Rebecca Shapiro : reconsideration of where you're placing that bike room... to prioritize sort of safe and convenient bike access... so we don't have... bikes... coming the wrong way down that drive aisle.

decisions.text: Simplify hotel street‑wall massing and window groupings to emphasize the key corner tower and reduce vertical segmentation, and strengthen the tower‑to‑lobby connection (e.g., lowering the awning).
decisions.source: Russell Sherman : the eye wants to do that... breaking this vertically... makes the eye do the opposite... bring the awning down... Tom Jess : simplifying the massing... adjust window grouping.

decisions.text: Enhance the office building’s lobby entry prominence and enrich the Ellis base with materials/landscape so the base aligns with the massing diagram and engages pedestrians.
decisions.source: Russell Sherman : the framed element is the prominent piece... the entry should be the focal point... Rebecca Shapiro : make the ground plane feel a little bit more special.

decisions.text: Adjust Building 2 south elevation by widening the center panel and pushing inverted L elements farther apart; refine the corner double‑reveal proportion for a cleaner rhythm.
decisions.source: Rebecca Shapiro : push those LS a little further apart... double reveal panel being a little bit atypical... Jonathan Thomas : we would take that opportunity... reduce this.

decisions.text: Evaluate replacing the purple accent with a darker, more saturated tone or a neutral dark gray during on‑site mock‑up, deciding final color with staff.
decisions.source: Rebecca Shapiro : evaluate the purple through a mock up... with an alternative... neutral, grey accent... Jonathan Thomas : I think it would be wonderful... if there was a recommendation that the purple went to a more neutral colour.

decisions.text: Ensure solar racks and all rooftop equipment remain below parapet height and set inboard; coordinate screening and utility layout with staff during permit to preserve planting and frontages.
decisions.source: Rebecca Shapiro : confirm... solar... not going to exceed... parapet... work closely with planning staff on the utility layout... Jonathan Thomas : those will definitely be lower than any roof screening.

decisions.text: Study setting the Building 2 security gate behind the building face and better aligning with the wrapped storefront corner so the building, not fencing, dominates the street view.
decisions.source: Rebecca Shapiro : make sure... the security gate... isn't forward of the building face... study whether it's... feasible to inset it further.

decisions.text: Work with staff on interim San Rafael frontage planting near the temporary office during Phase 1, or install Phase 2 tree plantings early if feasible, acknowledging future grading and sidewalk constraints.
decisions.source: Rebecca Shapiro : see the phase two... frontage planting incorporated into... phase one... barring that... work with staff on on interim planting along that frontage.

decisions.text: Add additional larger canopy trees along the highway sound wall where feasible and explore tree‑grate planting on the east sidewalk near Building 1, maintaining utility clearances.
decisions.source: Rebecca Shapiro : intersperse a tree that's a bit taller... explore a tree great style tree planting... make sure... transformer... can be planted there.
